questions.title: interface vs type — which should you use?
questions.shortAnswer: Both describe object shapes. Use interface for public object shapes you want to extend or merge; use type for unions, intersections, mapped, and conditional types.
questions.detailedExplanation: interfaces support declaration merging (handy for module augmentation). type aliases are more flexible for non-object shapes. Pick one as the default and stay consistent.
questions.realWorldExample: Library authors expose interfaces so consumers can augment them; app code often defaults to type for the extra power.
bestPractices: Pick a default, stick with it
Use type for unions and mapped types
commonMistakes: Trying to extend a union type with interface

codeSnippet.code:
interface User { id: string }
type Status = 'idle' | 'loading' | 'error'

questions.interviewTip: Mention declaration merging — a key interface-only feature.

questions.title: What are generics?
questions.shortAnswer: Generics let functions and types operate on values of any type while preserving the specific type at the call site.
questions.detailedExplanation: Generics turn types into parameters. Constraints (T extends U) keep them safe; defaults make them ergonomic.
questions.realWorldExample: Array<T>, Promise<T>, useState<T>() — all rely on generics so the return type matches what you put in.
bestPractices: Don't add generics if T is used only once
commonMistakes: Using any when a generic would do

codeSnippet.code:
function first<T>(arr: T[]): T | undefined { return arr[0] }
first([1,2,3]) // number | undefined

questions.interviewTip: Drop the phrase 'preserve type information across the boundary' — it sounds senior.

questions.title: unknown vs any.
questions.shortAnswer: any disables type checking; unknown is type-safe — you must narrow it before use.
questions.detailedExplanation: unknown is the recommended top type for data you don't trust (API responses, JSON.parse). Narrow with typeof, instanceof, or a Zod schema.
questions.realWorldExample: fetch().then(r => r.json()) returns Promise<any> — wrap with Zod to convert to a typed result.
bestPractices: Default to unknown for foreign data

codeSnippet.code: function isString(x: unknown): x is string { return typeof x === 'string' }

questions.interviewTip: Show a user-defined type guard — interviewers love them.

questions.title: What are utility types? Name a few.
questions.shortAnswer: Built-in generics that transform types: Partial<T>, Required<T>, Pick<T,K>, Omit<T,K>, Record<K,V>, ReturnType<F>, Awaited<T>.
questions.detailedExplanation: They reduce boilerplate and keep derived types in sync with their source. Composing them solves most real schema mapping problems.
questions.realWorldExample: type CreateUser = Omit<User, 'id' | 'createdAt'> models the input shape for a POST /users endpoint.
bestPractices: Use Pick/Omit to keep DTOs in sync with models

codeSnippet.code:
type User = { id: string; name: string }
type Update = Partial<Omit<User, 'id'>>

questions.interviewTip: Mention Awaited<T> — relatively new, signals you keep up.

questions.title: What are discriminated unions?
questions.shortAnswer: A union where each member shares a common literal field (the discriminant). Narrowing on that field tells TS which member you're in.
questions.detailedExplanation: Discriminants enable exhaustive checks via switch + never. They model state machines and API result shapes cleanly.
questions.realWorldExample: AsyncState<T> = { status: 'idle' } | { status: 'loading' } | { status: 'success'; data: T } | { status: 'error'; message: string }
commonMistakes: Falling back to any in default case
questions.interviewTip: Show the never-default exhaustive check — top-tier signal.
